I hope you enjoyed this article. Please feel free to share your inputs/suggestion/corrections in the comments sections.

Cheers

Ramesh Papanna

entry.title: Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC)
entry.content:
Hello Readers,

Hope you all are doing Great!

Today we will talk about the Advanced variant configuration used in SAP. The blog will give you a crisp and. clear understanding of the Advanced variant configuration.

  • What is Advanced variant configuration (AVC)?

Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC) is an evolution of the traditional Variant Configuration (VC) in SAP, designed to meet the needs of companies that manufacture highly configurable products. AVC extends the capabilities of the classic VC by offering more robust and flexible options for configuring products, especially in environments where products have a vast number of potential variants.

Industries use SAP Advanced Variant configuration: manufacturers of automobiles, personal computers, pumps, furniture, and so on.

  • Key Features of Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC):
  1. Simplified Model: AVC allows for simplified configuration models by reducing the number of dependencies and conditions needed.
  2. Flexible Data Model: AVC uses a more flexible data model that supports complex configurations without the need for extensive customization.
  3. Improved Performance: AVC is optimized for performance, especially in scenarios where there are large numbers of configurable products or where configuration needs to happen quickly, such as in e-commerce environments.
  4. Enhanced User Interface: The user interface in AVC is more intuitive, making it easier for users to navigate and configure products.
  5. Integration with SAP S/4HANA: AVC is fully integrated with SAP S/4HANA, providing seamless data flow and ensuring that configurations are consistent across all business processes.
  • Benefits of Using Advanced Variant Configuration:
  1. Good Customer experience.
  2. Increased Efficiency.
  3. Scalability: AVC is designed to handle large-scale configurations, making it suitable for companies that offer a wide range of configurable products.
  • Difference between Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC) and Classic Variant configuration (CVC):

Sr.no

Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC)

Classic Variant Configuration (CVC)

1

Built on SAP S/4HANA; optimized for HANA in-memory processing.

Built on older LO-VC technology; traditional SAP ECC-based.

2

Enhanced modelling with powerful constraint-based logic; supports complex rules and simulations.

Supports basic to intermediate configurations; uses dependency types like preconditions and selection conditions.

3

Modern Fiori-based UI with an intuitive and user-friendly experience.

Traditional SAP GUI interface.

4

Seamless integration with S/4HANA and modern applications; supports external system integration.

Limited integration capabilities; suited for legacy systems.

5

High performance even with complex configurations due to HANA optimization.

Performance may degrade with complex configurations.

6

Advanced features, including pricing integration and flexible configuration engine.

Basic configuration features with limited flexibility.

 

  • Step by step guide to implement Advanced variant configuration:

Business Requirement: Showroom sales bike having various variants such as bike colour, cubic capacity (cc), disc brake, while creating sales order there should be dependency of sequence on this variant such as without colour selection next variant cc should be gray out, the price range should depend on the model of various bike cc this is the business requirement.        

Step1:  a) Material can be partially configurable (Semi Finish & Finish) by maintaining below mentioned check

b) In Material master Sales: Sales org. 2 tab select Item category group as 002 (Configuration).

(Note: we do not need to open material in KMAT material type in Advance Variant Configuration.)

Step 2: Create Class, by using Transaction code- CL04

a) Enter the Class, Class type as 300, Valid from date and click on create button.

b) Enter Description, Status as Released and click on the save button.

Step 3: Create Characteristics by using transaction code: CT04.

a) Enter the characteristic as business requirement and click on the create button.

b) Enter Description, select status as Released, enter Data Type as CHAR (character Format), Number of characters: 10 (digits length), Check box the Entry required {Will not allow to save the sales order without entering the character (colour)}

 

C) Click on Additional data tab > Click on Values, Enter the Characteristic Values and its       description as below and click on save button.

Create the characteristics depend on business requirements, for this scenario I have created below characteristics and values to support my scenario.

Characteristics –

Colour- Red, white, yellow.

Cubic capacity- 100cc, 150cc, 200cc.

Disc brake- Single brake, Dual brake.

Step 4: Assign Characteristics to Class by using transactional code: CL04 in change mode:

a) Enter the Class created above and click on change button:

b) Assign the created characteristics and click on save button:

c) Saved the changes.

Step 5: Assign Class in Material by using transaction code: MM02:

a) Extend material to Classification tab and select class type- 300, enter the created class and click on save button:

Step 6: Assign configurable material to class:

a) Enter transaction code: CU41, select object material and click on continue button.

b) Enter Material and click on profile overview.

c) Configuration profile priority as 10, enter Prof. name, class type as 300 and select profile detail.

d) Click on Configuration initial screen tab and In Processing mode Select Advanced variant configuration and click on save button.

e) Configuration profile for material saved.

Step 7: Create Dependency using transaction code: CU01

a) Enter the dependency and press enter button. (Business Requirement is after entering bike colour system should give option to select only specific CC bikes).

b) Enter Description, In status select 1 (Released),In Processing mode select Advanced variant configuration and go Dependency editor and enter the Syntax and save the dependency.

Remark: If you need object dependency for the super BOM and super routing in your low-level configuration, you still need to use the Classic processing mode. Selection condition to select BOM items or routing operations still uses the LO-VC processing mode. BOM items and routing operations are part of the low-level configuration. The new Advanced processing mode is used in the Product Variant Configurator for high-level configuration.

If you want to use dependency, then below steps to be followed:

Step 8: Assign dependency to Characteristics by using transactional code: CT04

a) Enter characteristic and click on change button.

b) Click on Object Dependencies button.

c) Enter the dependency and click on Save button.

Step 9: Assign variant configuration standard condition “VA00” to pricing procedure in transaction code: V/08. (Price depend on business requirement)

Step 10: Add syntax object for characteristics using transaction code: CT04

Step 11: Maintain condition record for variant condition type- VA00 using transaction code: VK11 and save it.

Step 12:  Create Sales order

a) Login to GUI/FIORI and create sales order, enter the data below and press enter.

b) Characteristics value Assignment is opened with the dependencies created as below:

  • SAP Fiori- Simulate Configuration Models app is used for configurable products and configured objects and start a configuration simulation with them. Additionally, you can also search for and open a previously created and saved simulation. You can use the simulation to test and analyze your configuration model by configuring your object using the integrated characteristic value assignment screen and check whether your dependencies work. The app supports simulation for single-level and multi-level configurations.
     

  • The "Variant Configuration Overview" Fiori app provides an efficient way to manage and work with complex configurable products in SAP. It is designed for companies that use Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC) to offer customizable products to their customers. This app is part of SAP S/4HANA’s Advanced Variant Configuration solution, and it helps streamline product configuration, track options, and ensure compatibility.

There are Quick links to access various tasks on a click.

 

  • Multiple Characteristics can be restricted in AVC:

In AVC all characteristics are considered ‘Restrictable’. You can now restrict multiple value characteristics by using them in constraints.

 In Variant configuration you have to flag all the characteristics, you wanted to use in constraints as ‘restrictable’. For multiple value characteristics, however, this is impossible. The only way to create restrictions on multiple value characteristics is by using preconditions.

  • In “Manage characteristics group” app you can create new groups or assign existing groups. You determine how to group the characteristics, and how you want to sort them within the group.

In “Configuration profile” app the characteristics group can be reused in multiple configuration profiles (materials). We can sort the assigned characteristics and change the order in which the user can see.

Conclusion - Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC) in SAP S/4HANA enhances product configuration by leveraging the power of SAP's in-memory computing. It offers improved performance and scalability, handling complex and large-scale configurations with greater efficiency. AVC supports sophisticated rules and constraints, accommodating diverse and customizable product requirements. The modern, user-friendly interface simplifies configuration tasks and integrates seamlessly with SAP Fiori applications. It also provides robust integration with other SAP modules, ensuring cohesive management of processes from sales to production. By reducing complexity and minimizing custom developments, AVC streamlines implementation and supports end-to-end operational efficiency. This results in faster decision-making, improved user experience, and enhanced overall productivity.

entry.title: Was SAP UI Test Automation just killed by SAP API Hub and Solace?
entry.content:
Summary

In this blog I will try to explain a thesis on why SAP UI Test Automation is obsolete for most of SAP S/4HANA transformations and how did the Solace (Event Mesh) and SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com) might have killed it.

Chapter 1 - The problem with SAP UI testing

There are a few problems with SAP UI Test Automation but let's start with listing most typical SAP UI technologies used by SAP customers to access SAP:

- SAP GUI (with all it's versions) the traditional SAP Graphical User Interface used to interact with SAP ERP systems

- SAP Business Client - A desktop client that provides a unified access point to SAP Fiori, SAP GUI, and Web Dynpro applications

- SAP Fiori - the most modern and widely adopted UI technology for SAP applications

- SAPUI5 - SAPUI5 is a JavaScript-based UI development toolkit from SAP used for building responsive and feature-rich web applications

- Web Dynpro (yes still there) - A technology for developing web-based applications in SAP.

- SAP Mobile Technologies - to access SAP via mobile devices

- SAP CoPilot (future) - A digital assistant integrated into SAP applications, providing a conversational UI for interacting with SAP systems.


The big question is - which SAP UI technology you should use for test automation? The answer is simple, the one which is the most commonly used, right? What if most transactions are not being entered in a manual way (automated logistic systems) and most of the important ones (sales, purchasing, shipping) are coming via an interface (EDI - remember?).

If we are to summarise the biggest problems with SAP UI testing those would be:

a) difficulty to record a correct User Interface which keeps changing with each update of SAP business process, browser, etc.

b) very small number of people who can use the technology - only test automation teams (means it's never used by majority of real SAP Testers: functional teams, developers and business users)

c) creating SAP UI recordings is very complex - each transaction needs to be shown by a functional consultant and then the values can be used by the SAP Test Automation team


Chapter 2 - Changing the SAP landscapes - Solace (Even Mesh) and SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com)

With more and more composable ERP solutions, SAP being on the forefront of this innovation, has moved two concepts to a new level:

a) with the introduction of SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com) and other innovations around SAP Integration Suite - SAP S/4HANA can now connect to other applications on a completely different level via APIs and APIs have one significant advantage over SAP UI - they never change the interface. That means they are designed in way so if any change in the code happens the API will always work in the same way (unless a new version is released).

b) Solace partnership (Event Mesh and Advanced Event Mesh) - made another change, all of the business transactions can now easily output a message with it's content. That means getting the data from SAP transactions has become very simple and there's an abundance of that.

Having those two concepts (API Hub and Event-driven architecture - EDA) and corresponding technologies has just made it possible to skip doing SAP UI testing at all.

Chapter 3 - from SAP UI test automation to efficient SAP API test automation

Now that we can solve the 3 biggest issues with SAP UI Test Automation:

a) no need to record screens in different technologies and keep changing them if we can use SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com) and test the most important piece - an integrated SAP business process

b) expand the number of people who can do SAP Testing - real SAP Testers: functional teams, developers and business users can also run SAP API tests (never the case with UI automated testing). 

c) no need to ask SAP functional consultants to create sample data if event data is available and consumable by anyone in large quantities + they can be immediately used for testing with SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com)

Chapter 4 - How does it work now?

Imagine you have an Order to Cash (OTC) process where a sales order is entered using EDI and gets processed in a delivery and once the delivery is completed (by EDI) another steps would create an invoice. The old process would look like one from Figure 1. 

 

Figure 1 - old business process where UI test automation is used for manual steps in an Order to Cash process 

where most steps are done using API testing (EDI flows) but there are two (delivery and invoice creation) which needs to be done in a manual way using SAP UI testing solutions.

Now instead of that with the new way of working we can:

a) Find an API for the UI test on SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com)

Figure 2 - SAP Business Accelerator Hub with all available APIs  

b) find as many samples of data from manual transactions using Even Mesh notifications as possible

Figure 3 - sample notification message from SAP S/4HANA (goods movement example)  

c) use the data from b) and populate the API test call and create a business process sequence
in your SAP API test automation solution

Figure 4 - API workflow test of a complete business process using API calls only (example from - Int4 Suite)  

Chapter 5 - What Does It Mean?

The move away from traditional SAP UI testing towards API and event-driven testing brings a host of positive changes to the SAP landscape as shown in Figure 5.

Figure 5 - moving away from SAP UI automated testing into complete business process testing using API automated testing only 

This shift doesn't just introduce new tools or methodologies; it transforms how we approach testing and system integration as a whole. Here are the four most important implications of this transition:

1. Streamlined and Accurate Testing Processes
With SAP API testing, the testing process becomes more streamlined and accurate. APIs offer a consistent interface for testing, which means we no longer need to worry about the constant changes in the UI. This stability allows us to focus on validating the core business processes and data integrity rather than being bogged down by the specifics of each UI technology. In essence, testing becomes more about ensuring that the business logic works as intended, rather than dealing with the quirks and changes of different user interfaces.

2. Wider Testing Participation Across Teams
One of the key benefits of shifting to API testing is that it opens up the testing process to a broader range of people within the organization. Unlike traditional UI testing, which often required specialized skills and tools, API testing can be conducted by functional teams, developers, and even business users. This democratization of testing means more comprehensive coverage, as those who understand the business processes best can now directly engage in testing activities. It eliminates the bottleneck of relying solely on test automation teams and allows for a more collaborative approach.

3. Faster and More Agile Deployment Cycles
By eliminating the need for complex and time-consuming UI testing, we can significantly accelerate the deployment cycle. APIs, by their nature, are less prone to frequent changes, making them easier and faster to test. This agility means that organizations can adapt to changes in their business environment more quickly, deploying updates and new features with confidence. In the fast-paced world of SAP S/4HANA transformations, this speed is a critical advantage, helping companies stay ahead of the competition.

4. Cost Savings and Resource Optimization
Moving away from UI testing can lead to considerable cost savings. Traditional SAP UI testing often requires expensive tools, skilled automation engineers, and significant time investment. By leveraging APIs and event-driven architectures, we can cut down on these costs. Testing at the API level requires fewer resources and can be executed by a broader group of people, making it more cost-effective. Furthermore, this shift allows organizations to allocate their resources more efficiently, focusing on enhancing their core business processes instead of maintaining complex UI test scripts.

In Conclusion

The decline of SAP UI testing, brought on by the rise of the SAP API portal and Solace's event-driven architecture, represents a fundamental shift in how we approach SAP testing. It's not merely about replacing one tool with another; it's about adopting a more efficient, inclusive, and agile way of ensuring system integrity. By focusing on API and event-driven testing, organizations can streamline their testing processes, involve more people in meaningful ways, accelerate their deployment cycles, and save costs. This evolution aligns testing more closely with business objectives, ensuring that what really matters—the core business processes—are thoroughly validated and optimized.

Further Reference: 

Avoid SAP S/4HANA Project Delays with Third-Party Systems Service Virtualization

entry.title: Unlocking Efficiency: Choosing the Ideal SAP Warehousing Solution for Your Company
entry.content:
In today's dynamic supply chain landscape, businesses require efficient and flexible warehouse management systems (WMS) to stay competitive. SAP offers robust solutions for optimizing warehouse operations. However, with multiple options available, such as Basic EWM, Advanced EWM, Decentralized EWM, and newer alternatives like SAP Stock Room Management and SAP Warehouse Management on S/4HANA Public Cloud, choosing the right one can be challenging.

This blog will explore each of these options and provide guidance on selecting the most suitable solution for your business.

Understanding the Different SAP Warehouse Management Options

1. SAP Stock Room Management

  • What It Is: Stock Room Management is a simplified warehouse management solution introduced within SAP S/4HANA. It offers core warehouse functionalities tailored for businesses with straightforward warehouse needs. SAP created Stock Room Management for customers migrating from SAP’s classic Warehouse Management (WM) system to S/4HANA, but who do not require the advanced features of SAP EWM.
  • Key Features:
    • Core warehouse processes like stock transfers, goods receipt, and goods issue.
    • Basic inventory management functionalities.
    • Material handling, cycle counting, and internal movements.
    • Simplified user experience with integration into SAP S/4HANA.
  • Best Suited For: Businesses that require basic warehouse functionalities without the need for advanced automation or complex workflows. It is ideal for smaller warehouses or companies transitioning from SAP’s classic Warehouse Management system who want to stay within the SAP S/4HANA ecosystem without upgrading to full EWM.

2. SAP Warehouse Management on S/4HANA Public Cloud

  • What It Is: SAP Warehouse Management (WM) on S/4HANA Public Cloud provides cloud-based warehouse management functionalities for businesses that want to move their operations to the cloud. It offers core WM features while leveraging the scalability and flexibility of the public cloud infrastructure.
  • Key Features:
    • Cloud-based deployment with access to core warehouse functionalities.
    • Seamless integration with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.
    • Real-time inventory visibility and warehouse operations.
    • Regular updates from SAP without the need for heavy customizations.
  • Best Suited For: Businesses looking for a scalable, cloud-based solution to manage basic warehouse operations, especially those that want to reduce infrastructure costs and simplify system management. It is an excellent option for companies transitioning to the cloud or that want a more agile and scalable warehouse management solution.

3. Basic EWM (Embedded within SAP S/4HANA)

  • What It Is: Basic EWM is the foundational version of SAP EWM, embedded within SAP S/4HANA. It provides essential warehouse management features with no additional licensing costs.
  • Key Features:
    • Basic warehouse processes such as picking, packing, and goods receipt/goods issue.
    • Inventory management and stock transfers.
    • Integration with SAP S/4HANA for seamless data flow.
  • Best Suited For: Small to medium-sized businesses or warehouses with simple processes that don’t require advanced features like automation or complex workflows.

4. Advanced EWM (Embedded within SAP S/4HANA)

  • What It Is: Advanced EWM is the more feature-rich version of SAP EWM, offering a comprehensive set of advanced warehouse management functionalities within SAP S/4HANA.
  • Key Features:
    • Complex warehouse operations, including cross-docking, wave management, slotting, and labor management.
    • Full support for automation systems (AGVs, conveyors).
    • Advanced stock management, including batch management and serialization.
    • Yard management, kitting, and value-added services.
  • Best Suited For: Medium to large businesses with complex warehouse processes that require high levels of automation and optimization.

5. Decentralized EWM

  • What It Is: Decentralized EWM is a stand-alone version of SAP EWM that operates on a separate server from the SAP ERP or SAP S/4HANA system, offering greater flexibility and scalability.
  • Key Features:
    • Runs independently from the SAP ERP system for improved flexibility.
    • Comprehensive functionality similar to Advanced EWM.
    • Designed for large warehouses with high transaction volumes and complex operations.
    • Multi-warehouse support and real-time process control.
  • Best Suited For: Large, global enterprises with multiple warehouses, complex operations, and high transaction volumes, where independence from the ERP system is crucial for uninterrupted operations.

Key Factors to Consider When Choosing the Right SAP Warehouse Management Solution

1. Warehouse Complexity

  • Stock Room Management and Basic EWM are designed for businesses with straightforward warehouse operations. They are ideal if your processes are mostly manual, and you don’t require complex workflows or advanced automation.
  • Advanced EWM and Decentralized EWM offer more comprehensive features, such as integration with automation, real-time process monitoring, and optimization. These are best for businesses with intricate warehouse setups, multiple locations, or advanced inventory management needs.
  • SAP WM on S/4HANA Public Cloud is suitable for companies seeking a cloud-first approach, providing core functionalities but with scalability and real-time access to inventory data.

2. Automation Needs

  • If your warehouse relies on automation or is planning to integrate automated systems (e.g., conveyors, AGVs, robots), you will need the capabilities of Advanced EWM or Decentralized EWM. These solutions offer robust support for integrating automation with your warehouse processes.
  • Stock Room Management and Basic EWM are better suited for manual operations or warehouses with low levels of automation.

3. Cloud Adoption Strategy

  • SAP Warehouse Management on S/4HANA Public Cloud is the optimal choice if your business is looking to adopt a cloud-based WMS. It provides flexibility, regular updates, and reduced infrastructure costs, making it ideal for companies focused on digital transformation and scalability.
  • For businesses that prefer an on-premise system or already have SAP S/4HANA in place, Embedded EWM (Basic or Advanced) or Decentralized EWM are viable options.

4. Cost and Licensing

  • Stock Room Management and Basic EWM are included with SAP S/4HANA, making them cost-effective solutions for businesses that don’t need the additional features provided by Advanced or Decentralized EWM.
  • Advanced EWM requires additional licensing, but it offers significantly more functionality for businesses with complex warehouse needs.
  • Decentralized EWM comes with higher costs, as it requires separate infrastructure and licensing, but it is essential for large enterprises that need a fully independent warehouse management system.

5. Future Growth and Scalability

  • Stock Room Management and Basic EWM are suitable for businesses with relatively stable warehouse operations that don’t anticipate significant changes in the near future.
  • Advanced EWM and Decentralized EWM offer scalability, making them ideal for growing businesses or enterprises with plans to expand their warehouse operations, implement automation, or add new distribution centers.
  • SAP Warehouse Management on S/4HANA Public Cloud provides cloud-native scalability, making it an excellent choice for companies looking to grow their operations quickly and cost-effectively while leveraging cloud infrastructure.

Conclusion: Which SAP Warehouse Management Solution is Right for You?

Choosing the right SAP warehouse management solution depends on your business’s operational complexity, growth plans, and technological direction. Here's a summary of when each solution may be the best fit:

  • Stock Room Management: Best for businesses with simple warehouse operations that are moving from SAP’s classic Warehouse Management (WM) system and want to continue with basic functionality within SAP S/4HANA.
  • SAP Warehouse Management on S/4HANA Public Cloud: Ideal for companies focused on adopting cloud solutions with core warehouse functionality and scalability.
  • Basic EWM: Perfect for small to medium-sized businesses that need essential warehouse management features as part of their SAP S/4HANA system without additional costs.
  • Advanced EWM: Suited for medium to large businesses with more complex warehouse operations, requiring advanced automation, inventory management, and process optimization.
  • Decentralized EWM: The best choice for large, multi-warehouse businesses that need a fully independent WMS, capable of handling high volumes and offering real-time, 24/7 operations.

By assessing your current and future warehouse needs, transaction volumes, levels of automation, and growth strategy, you can make an informed decision and choose the right SAP warehouse management solution to drive operational efficiency and support your business objectives.

entry.title: Adapting Launchpad App Descriptor Items via adaptation mode in Launchpad App Manager
entry.content:
Objective:

The purpose of this blog is to demonstrate how to modify the app descriptor items in SAP-delivered technical catalogs using the adaptation mode in the Launchpad App Manager.

The adaptation mode in the Launchpad App Manager allows us to modify the following fields:

Group of Fields

Field

Target Application Fields

  • Target Application Title
  • Target Mapping Information
  • Device Type - Desktop, Tablet, Phone

Tile Fields

  • Tile Title
  • Tile Subtitle
  • Tile Icon
  • Tile Keywords
  • Tile Information
  • Refresh Interval in Seconds

 

Requirement:

The requirement is to add a 'Tile Subtitle' and 'Tile Icon' to the standard Fiori app 'Customers Overview'.

 

Steps:

Now, let's walk through the steps to achieve the desired adaptation:

  • Open the 'Customers Overview' app in the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library and identify the Technical Catalog ID associated with the app

  • Open the SAP Fiori Launchpad Application Manager app, either directly or by using transaction code ‘/UI2/FLPAM_ADAPT’

         

  • Open the Technical Catalog ID ‘SAP_TC_CEC_SD_COMMON’

        

  • In the technical catalog, select the relevant SAP Fiori ID from the list and access the details view. You can search for it as follows

         

         

        

  • Switch to adaptation mode by clicking on ‘Switch to Adaptation Mode’.

         

  • To make changes to the relevant items, enter edit mode

          

  • Enter the transport request (TR) details and navigate to the Adaptation Tab.

          

         

  • Perform the required changes by checking the Adapt checkbox for the fields you want to modify. In this case, enter the details for ‘Tile Subtitle’ and ‘Tile Icon’.         
  • To obtain the icon details, go to the SAP Icon Explorer and search for a suitable icon.          

 

  • Copy the icon details as highlighted in the screenshot below. These details will be entered in the adaptation view.         
  • Enter the required details in the adaptation view as follows: 

Results:

Open the Fiori Launchpad and test the ‘Customers Overview’ app. All the changes you applied should now be displayed.

entry.title: Where can I start learning the all about the ey products of SAP Intelligent Asset Management (IAM)?
entry.content:

Margit

entry.title: Embedding Generative AI into SAP S/4HANA: A Holistic Approach to Intelligent Business Operations
entry.content:
In the fast-paced world of enterprise technology, Generative AI is making waves by offering capabilities far beyond traditional AI. In SAP S/4HANA, this evolution is driving a new era of transactional intelligence where AI not only generates text or automates workflows but actively engages with real-time data and enterprise processes to enhance business operations.

In this post, we’ll explore how Generative AI is embedded into SAP S/4HANA, transforming how businesses interact with their core systems, from automating content creation to making informed transactional decisions. This is based on the latest advancements highlighted by SAP’s own Generative AI solution concepts and expanding use cases​.

Generative AI: Moving from Content to Transactions

Generative AI traditionally started as a tool for text generation, handling tasks like content creation, summarization, or code generation. However, in the context of SAP, Generative AI’s role has expanded dramatically.

We now see GenAI being applied to a wider range of processes:

  1. Text -> Code -> Workflow -> Transactional Data: Generative AI in SAP now supports generating not just textual outputs but handling workflows and interacting with transactional data. Whether it's automating invoice generation, managing inventory, or creating product orders, GenAI can handle the entire flow autonomously, offering solutions to business challenges as they arise​.
  2. Prompting Large Language Models (LLMs) for Enhanced Business Interaction:
    • LLM Retrieval via Fine-Tuning and RAG: Through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), businesses can now send SAP data into LLMs via HANA vector databases to receive precise responses based on real-time transactional information. Imagine asking the system, "Can this sales order be fulfilled by the end of the month?" and getting a direct, data-backed response​.
    • Document Processing through OCR and LLMs: GenAI can take unstructured data, such as scanned PDFs, and transform it into structured formats like JSON, automating document management processes like invoice extraction or contract analysis​.

These advancements enable non-technical users to interact with SAP’s intelligent core using natural language prompts, making complex business operations more intuitive and efficient.

Expanding LLM Capabilities for SAP S/4HANA

One of the most exciting advancements is the ability to extend LLMs to act as intelligent agents within the SAP environment. LLMs are no longer passive tools but can now plan, execute, and optimize business processes autonomously.

Key innovations include:

  1. LLM-Driven SAP API Integration: Generative AI is integrated with SAP APIs, allowing LLMs to autonomously retrieve API data, plan business actions (such as initiating a production order), and even execute these actions without human intervention​.
    • Example: The LLM accesses the SAP API_CORE, retrieves available APIs, and autonomously configures business processes such as setting up an order, managing inventory, or scheduling deliveries. This level of intelligent automation reduces manual intervention and accelerates business decisions.
  2. Four-Tier LLM Structure for Enhanced Enterprise Automation:
    • General-purpose LLMs: Handle generic language and data tasks.
    • Domain-specific LLMs: Fine-tuned with SAP data, these models can understand complex enterprise data and processes.
    • Cross-task LLMs: Perform more specialized functions like job posting creation, purchase order generation, and workflow automation.
    • Task-specific LLMs: Fully customized for specific business tasks, such as generating API calls for unique operational needs​(Embedding Generative AI…).

This hierarchical model structure offers both broad and specialized AI capabilities, empowering businesses to customize their AI solutions for optimal performance. A detailed blog is available on LAM (Large Action Model) powered agents for enterprise process automation to execute a bunch of tasks.

Building SAP’s Own Generative AI Foundation Models

SAP is moving toward building its own Generative AI foundation models, specifically designed to handle the unique challenges of enterprise environments. These models are not just about handling text or data—they integrate business process context, industry-specific knowledge, and transactional intelligence.

SAP’s own foundation models are trained on structured data, such as financial transactions, sales orders, and manufacturing records, as well as tabular and graph-based data. This integration allows businesses to query real-time transactional data for more accurate, predictive decision-making.

For example:

  • Predictive Transactional Intelligence: Imagine asking the system, “Will this product shipment arrive on time based on current production schedules?” SAP’s GenAI model, trained on past transactional data and current operational insights, will deliver a reliable prediction​.

SAP’s approach focuses on integrating these foundation models with business process metadata, creating a knowledge graph that continuously improves AI accuracy and relevance in real-time business contexts.

Solution Architecture: Embedding Generative AI in SAP S/4HANA

SAP’s Generative AI solution architecture is built on a scalable and adaptable framework. The architecture leverages SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) to provide advanced capabilities in prompt engineering, embedding lookup, and model fine-tuning, all of which are key to customizing the AI to the needs of each enterprise.

Key components of the architecture include:

  1. Joule Integration: Joule is a natural language interface that helps users retrieve information or execute tasks through Conversational AI. The assistant interacts with the Generative AI models to provide real-time business insights​. A detailed blog will be available soon on Joule implementation.
  2. Advanced Prompting: For applications requiring complex prompt engineering, SAP’s BTP provides capabilities such as embedding domain-specific knowledge or leveraging vector search engines to improve the relevance of AI outputs. This ensures that the prompts provided to the LLMs are fine-tuned to yield the most accurate responses​.

Model Retraining for Custom Business Needs: SAP’s architecture supports model retraining using specific enterprise data. This allows businesses to continuously improve their AI models to match evolving operational requirements​.

Conclusion: Generative AI and the Future of SAP S/4HANA

The integration of Generative AI into SAP S/4HANA marks a turning point in the evolution of enterprise AI. With SAP building its own foundation models, capable of handling not just content but transactional and predictive business intelligence, the potential for businesses to automate, optimize, and innovate has never been greater.

From intelligent document processing to real-time decision-making and workflow automation, Generative AI is not only enhancing how enterprises operate but is paving the way for the future of the intelligent enterprise.

If you haven’t explored the potential of embedding Generative AI into your SAP environment, now is the time. Whether you're looking to automate processes, improve decision-making, or simply enhance user experiences, SAP S/4HANA is ready to help you unlock the full potential of this transformative technology.

entry.title: How to restrict access to material types and material groups in SAP S4 HANA
entry.content:
Restricting access to material types and material groups in SAP S4 HANA 

In S4 HANA Procurement, material types and material groups are restricted using authorization objects M_MATE_MAR (Material Master: Material Types) and M_MATE_WGR (Material Master: Material Groups). Both these objects have a common field value called BEGRU (Authorization group).

Material types and material groups cannot be directly maintained in BEGRU. First, use the Tcode SE54 to create authorization groups. The authorization groups must then be mapped to material types and groups using the T-codes OMS2 and OMSF. Then these authorization groups should be maintained in a role.

Note: Material types with no authorization group will skip the M_MATE_MAR authority check. material groups with no authorization group will skip M_MATE_WGR. Hence, authorization groups should be maintained against all the material types (allowed and restricted) and material groups (allowed and restricted) to make the access restrictions work.

 

Authorization object

Description

Field1

Description

Field2

Description

M_MATE_MAR

(Material Master: Material Types)

Actvt

Activity

BEGRU

Authorization group

M_MATE_WGR

(Material Master: Material Groups)

Actvt

Activity

BEGRU

Authorization group

Let us look at an example of restricting users' access to specific material types and groups while using the T-codes MM01, MM02, and MM03.

Material types allowed for users – Goods. All the other material types other than Goods should be restricted for users.

Material groups allowed for users - Finance Services. All the other material groups other than Finance Services should be restricted for users.

Two sets of authorization groups should be created in SE54 for the case described above.

Z001 for allowed material types.

Z002 for restricted material types.

Z003 for allowed material groups.

Z004 for restricted material groups.

Steps:

  • Create authorization groups for material types and material groups using the Tcode SE54.

  • Map authorization groups with the material types using the Tcode OMS2 (maintain the authorization group Z001 against the allowed material types (Goods) and maintain the authorization group Z002 against all the restricted material types.)

  • Map authorization groups with the material groups using the T-code OMSF (maintain the authorization group Z003 against all the allowed material groups (Finance Services) and maintain the authorization group Z004 against all the restricted material groups.)

  • Maintain the authorization groups in the authorization objects (M_MATE_MAR and M_MATE_WGR) in PFCG.

entry.title: Hey! Have you heard of SAP University Alliances// SAP Next-Gen?
entry.content:
Become a member of the SAP University Alliances program and enhance your student's academic outcome. Collaborate on research projects and get access to SAP software, teaching materials and related support.

Dear Beginner Corner visitor, 

Either you are a student, professor, lecturer or are interested in learning about SAP, have you visited our SAP University Alliances webpage? 

About SAP University Alliances

The SAP University Alliances program enables academia to educate the next generation for the Intelligent Enterprise and the experience economy, engage at SAP events, build industry partnerships, and prepare graduates for the SAP ecosystem.

  • Teach hands-on with SAP technology at your university 

  • Participate in strategic research activities to build high-impact collaborations to educate the leaders of tomorrow

  • Foster close partnerships with the academic community through regional academic boards and educational institutions worldwide

In case you want to stayed tuned, check out for news in our SAP University Alliances community - public group for Latin America & Caribbean, Spain and Portugal where share events for academics, for students, free software access for academic usage, and further projects and initiatives. 

See you there! 

Best regards,

SAP University Alliances - LAC, Spain and Portugal

entry.title: Service Confirmation - Using Defined Logistics Scenario for Material Withdrawal
entry.content:
This blog will explore service confirmations using defined logistics scenarios for material withdrawal. There are primarily two types of withdrawals: one using Plant / Storage Location and another using Technician Consignment. Let's start with the example of withdrawal using a Plant / Storage Location

In a service scenario, suppose a piece of equipment needs to be serviced, and a service order is created. Once the service order is released for further processing, you can proceed with the following steps. Based on the configuration mentioned below, a reservation will be generated.

Path SPRO-> Service-> Transactions-> Setting for Service Transactions-> Integration-> Logistics Integration-> Organizational Management Legacy-> Define Logistics Scenario for Procurement of Materials and Services.

In this step, enter the required details such as the transaction type and item category, and select the logistics scenario as "Reservation."

Let's consider a spare part with a stock quantity of 30.

Once a service order is created with a spare part quantity of 4, release the service order. A reservation will then be generated for the 4 quantities.

In the transaction history, the reservation number will be displayed once it is successfully generated.

In the MMBE (Stock Overview) screen, you can view the same reservation details, including the reserved quantity and stock availability.

Next, you will create a follow-up and initiate the service confirmation.

 

Service confirmation are particularly useful because they allow you to confirm the use of spare parts. For instance, if the service order reserved four spare parts but only three were actually used, you can adjust the quantity in the service confirmation to reflect the usage of only three. After updating the quantity, save the service confirmation. Once saved, change the status to "Completed" to generate the material document based on below mentioned config.

Path SPRO-> Service-> Transactions-> Setting for Service Transactions-> Integration-> Logistics Integration-> Organizational Management Legacy-> Define Logistics Scenario for Material Withdrawal.

In this step, enter the required details such as the transaction type and item category, and select the logistics scenario as "P Plant / Storage Location."

Here I mentioned only 3 quantities.

The system will automatically issue the goods based on the reservation with the quantity specified in the service confirmation. In the transaction history, the Goods movement number will be displayed once it is successfully generated.

Open the document in MIGO.

Automatic goods issue happened only for quantity mentioned in service confirmation.

In the MMBE (Stock Overview) screen, you can view the same, including the reserved quantity and stock availability.

From 30 it is reduced to 27 in unrestricted stock; and from 4 it is reduced to 1 in reserved stock. Any remaining quantity can be returned to unrestricted stock using the MIGO transaction.

Return delivery against Reservation.

In the MMBE (Stock Overview) screen, you can view the updated details, including the reserved quantity and stock availability. After processing, the reserved quantity will be reduced to zero, and the unrestricted stock will increase to 28 (reflecting the return of 1 qty  from the reserved stock).

 

Now, Let's examine material withdrawal using Technician - Consignment. In this case, if equipment that needs servicing is installed in the field, the technician must travel to that location to perform the service. This scenario is often referred to as field service. To manage this, the technician will need to bring along some spare parts, and this withdrawal method helps track those parts.

Before proceeding, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  1. Extend the technician  to Customer role in the Business Partner (BP) transaction.
  2. For the extended customer (Technician), create a consignment order using the VA01 transaction code with the order type KB (Consignment Fill-up).

Now, if you check, you'll see that there are 6 QTY available in the customer consignment stock.

In the above image, this stock is allocated to the Technician who will be going to the field to perform the service.

    3. Since we are withdrawing the material using consignment, reservations are not needed. Therefore, remove the relevant configuration.

Once all the prerequisites are completed, create a service order with a quantity of 5 and release the service order.

In the transaction history, you will see that no reservations have been generated.

 

Now, you need to create service confirmations to confirm the quantity. Since the technician went to the field with 6 quantities, the goods movement should be processed against those 6 quantities (from the consignment stock). For the automatic goods issue, the following configuraton needs to be done:

Path SPRO-> Service-> Transactions-> Setting for Service Transactions-> Integration-> Logistics Integration-> Organizational Management Legacy-> Define Logistics Scenario for Material Withdrawal.

In this step, enter the required details such as the transaction type and item category, and select the logistics scenario as "T Technician - Consignment."  with special stock indicator ‘W’.

Now, create the service confirmation and change the status to "Completed." This will finalize the service confirmation and trigger the necessary goods movement.

In the transaction history, the Goods movement number will be displayed once it is successfully generated.

Open the document in MIGO transaction to view the consignment issue against the technician, reflecting the material withdrawal from the consignment stock.

In the MMBE (Stock Overview) screen, you can view the same, including the Consignment stock and stock availability.

 

 

 

 

 

 

In this way, service confirmation plays a crucial role in automating material withdrawal from inventory. Thank you for taking the time to read the blog—I hope you found it helpful and informative!

SAP S/4HANA, SAP Field Service Management,  SAP S/4HANA Finance, SAP CRM Service Manager,  SAP CRM on-demand solution

entry.title: Quality Management with SAP Batch Release Hub and SAP S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
In the ever-evolving pharmaceutical industry, the need for efficient and compliant batch release processes is key. Roche, a global leader in healthcare and pharmaceuticals, has taken significant strides in enhancing their quality management systems by leveraging SAP Batch Release Hub and SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The approach not only streamlines operations but also ensures adherence to stringent regulatory standards.

This was the topic of the episode 109 of the Podcast Inside SAP S/4HANA Cloud: Episode 109: Roche’s Quality Management Strategy with SAP Batch Release Hub and SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Check more details below!

 

The Power of Collaboration: Roche and SAP Co-Innovation

The journey of transforming Roche's batch release process began with a vision to integrate and automate quality management using SAP’s cutting-edge technology. The collaboration has the participation of Melanie Fabian and Dr. Marco Strohmeier from Roche, along with Aladdin Mandishah from SAP, who brought a wealth of industry knowledge and technical expertise to the table. They are the guests of the Episode 109, hosted by Markus Oertelt, product expert at SAP.

- Melanie Fabian: With a background in pharmacy and experience in pharmaceutical manufacturing and quality management, Melanie Fabian helped in defining the technical requirements and ensuring the system met regulatory expectations.

- Dr. Marco Strohmeier: Strohmeier's expertise in biotech manufacturing and quality, combined with process optimization, helped shaping the project’s direction towards a fully integrated quality management system.

- Aladdin Mandishah: As the director of product marketing in SAP’s life sciences and healthcare unit, Mandishah focused on aligning the solution with industry needs and promoting the tool within the sector.

 

Challenges and Innovations in Batch Release and Quality Management

The collaboration aimed to address several challenges inherent in the traditional batch release processes, such as data integration from multiple systems, compliance with global standards, and the need for automation to reduce manual checks.

Integration with Multiple Systems: Roche required a solution that could consolidate data from various external systems essential for batch release, such as quality control, deviation management, and regulatory compliance systems.

Automation and Compliance: The SAP Batch Release Hub was designed to automate critical checks and balances, reducing the reliance on manual processes and ensuring compliance with international standards.

 

The Outcome: A Flexible and Robust System

The co-innovation resulted in the creation of the SAP Batch Release Hub, a flexible tool tailored to meet the diverse needs of Roche’s production processes. This system enables Roche to manage different types of material releases through customizable workflows, ensuring efficiency and compliance across the board.

Material-Specific Workflows: The tool accommodates various release processes, from drug substances to production components, by allowing customization according to material type.

Adaptability to Business Needs: The SAP Batch Release Hub is designed to adapt to different business requirements and regulatory environments, ensuring that it can be deployed globally while meeting local compliance demands.

Learn more about SAP Batch Release Hub

 

Looking Ahead: Artificial Intelligence in Quality Management

While the current focus is on streamlining and integrating batch release processes, future enhancements could include the application of artificial intelligence (AI). AI has the potential to further improve efficiency by automating data interpretation and decision-making processes, particularly in areas like deviation management and supply chain optimization.

Check the GenAI-assisted Batch Release - Phase II at SAP Road Map Explorer

 

Tune into the Innovation Journey

To gain deeper insights into how Roche and SAP are pioneering advancements in pharmaceutical quality management, listen to the full episode of the podcast Inside SAP S/4HANA Cloud. Discover firsthand accounts from Melanie Fabian, Dr. Marco Strohmeier, and Aladdin Mandishah on the challenges, innovations, and successes of this project. This episode not only sheds light on the technical aspects of the SAP Batch Release Hub but also explores the collaborative efforts that made this innovation possible.

Listen to the Episode Now and explore how cutting-edge technology is shaping the future of pharmaceutical manufacturing and quality assurance.

 

Podcast Inside SAP S/4HANA Cloud

There is no customer success without project success and product success! Leverage the unique knowledge and expertise of SAP S/4HANA Cloud experts, partners, and customers, sharing their SAP S/4HANA Cloud product insights, implementation project best practices and lessons learned – anywhere, anytime. In this podcast, we give you all ingredients to get to the next level and make your SAP S/4HANA Cloud project a success. Check all the episodes at sap.com/podcasts, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, TuneIn, and more audio platforms.

entry.title: How to setup SAP CDC Solution in Azure Data Factory
entry.content:
Introduction

Azure Data Factory (ADF) is a data integration (ETL/ELT) Platform as a Service (PaaS) and for SAP data integration, ADF currently offers six connectors:

These connectors can only extract data in batches, where each batch treats old and new data equally w/o identifying data changes (“batch mode”).  This extraction mode isn’t optimal when dealing w/ large data sets, such as tables w/ millions/billions of records, that change often 

Consequently, customers have been asking for a new connector that can extract only data changes (inserts/updates/deletes = “deltas”), leveraging Change Data Capture (CDC) feature that exists in most SAP systems (“CDC mode”).  After gathering their requirements, New SAP CDC connector leveraging SAP Operational Data Provisioning (ODP) framework.  This new connector can connect to all SAP systems that support ODP, such as R/3, ECC, S/4HANA, BW, and BW/4HANA, directly at the application layer or indirectly via SAP Landscape Transformation (SLT) replication server as a proxy.  It can fully/incrementally extract SAP data that includes not only physical tables, but also logical objects created on top of those tables, such as ABAP) Core Data Services (CDS) views,

Architecture

The high-level architecture of our SAP CDC solution in ADF is divided into two sides, left-hand-side (LHS) and right-hand-side (RHS).  LHS includes SAP CDC connector that invokes ODP API over standard Remote Function Call (RFC) modules to extract raw SAP data (full + deltas).  RHS includes ADF copy activity that loads the raw SAP data into any destination, such as Azure Blob Storage/Azure Data Lake Store (ADLS) Gen2, in CSV/Parquet format, essentially archiving/preserving all historical changes.  RHS can also include ADF data flow activity that transforms the raw SAP data, merges all changes, and loads the result into any destination, such as Azure SQL Database/Azure Synapse Analytics, essentially replicating SAP data.  ADF data flow activity can also load the result into ADLS Gen2 in Delta format, enabling time-travel to produce snapshots of SAP data at any given periods in the past.  LHS and RHS can be combined as SAP CDC/replication template to auto-generate ADF pipeline that can be frequently run using ADF tumbling window trigger to replicate SAP data into Azure w/ low latency and w/o watermarking.

 

ADF copy activity w/ SAP CDC connector runs on a self-hosted integration runtime (SHIR) that you install on your on-premises/virtual machine, so it has a line of sight to your SAP source systems/SLT replication server, while ADF data flow activity runs on a serverless Databricks/Spark cluster, Azure IR.  SAP CDC connector via ODP can extract various data source (“provider”) types, such as:

 

  • SAP extractors, originally built to extract data from SAP ECC and load it into SAP BW
  • ABAP CDS views, the new data extraction standard for SAP S/4HANA
  • Info Providers and Info Objects in SAP BW and BW/4HANA
  • SAP application tables, when using SLT replication server as a proxy

 

These providers run on SAP systems to produce full/incremental data in Operational Delta Queue (ODQ) that is consumed by ADF copy activity w/ SAP CDC connector in ADF pipeline (“subscriber”).

INITIAL SLT CONFIGURATION

SLT is a database trigger-enabled CDC solution that can replicate SAP application tables and simple views in near real time. SLT replicates from SAP source systems to various targets, including the operational delta queue (ODQ). In S/4HANA 2020 and higher, the addon is already built into the S4CORE component.

During the replication process, database triggers track all changes to the data stored in the source tables. Every operation is registered in the logging table, and the function module transfers data to a specified target. SLT automatically creates all required objects when you initiate the data extraction. Keeping changes in the logging tables provides a level of fault tolerance that prevents data loss when the system that manages the replication is temporarily unavailable. In such a case, once the replication process is re-established, the SLT can easily identify all not-replicated changes and continue operation.


To start the replication process you need to set-up SAP SLT. You do it by creating configurations that controls the replication process. You manage the configuration in the SLT Cockpit (t-code: LTRC). In the Standalone deployment you should start the cockpit on the Replication Server, and not on the source.

The initial screen of the SLT Cockpit lists all previously created configurations. Each configuration has a unique identifier called Mass Transfer ID (MTID). Click the Create Configuration button to define a new one. It opens a wizard that guides you through basic settings.

The SAP SLT framework is part of the DMIS (Data Migration Server) component. In standalone deployment, you must install it on both systems: the source (transactional) system, as well as the one

The number of Data Transfer / Initial Load / Calculation Jobs should come from the sizing you perform. For small configuration, with limited number of tables in scope SAP recommends using not less than two data load jobs. There is 1-to-1 correlation between specified data transfer jobs and the dialog work processes in the source system.

The number of Initial Load Jobs should be lower than total number of data transfer jobs, otherwise after the initial load the system won’t replicate changes. Calculation Jobs run the initial assessment of the data stored in the source table and chunk data into smaller pieces.

Validation

To validate your SAP system configurations for ODP, you can run RODPS_REPL_TEST program to test the extraction of your SAPI extractors, CDS views, BW objects, etc.

Prepare SHIR (self-hosted integration runtime) w/ SAP CDC connector

To prepare SHIR w/ SAP CDC connector, complete the following steps: \

  1. On ADF Studio, create and configure SHIR, We can download our latest private SHIR version w/ improved performance and detailed error messages from https://adfsapfileshare.blob.core.windows.net/shir/IntegrationRuntime_5.18.8172.1.msi and install it on your on-premises/virtual machine.
  2. The more CPU cores you have on your SHIR machine, the higher your data extraction throughput.
  3. To avoid being replaced by later versions, on ADF Studio, go to the Integration runtimes section of Manage hub, select your SHIR to edit, and select the Auto update tab to disable this feature.

Download the latest 64-bit SAP .NET Connector (SAP NCo 3.0) from https://support.sap.com/en/product/connectors/msnet.html and install it on your SHIR machine.  During installation, select the Install Assemblies to GAC option in the Optional setup steps window. 

 

Add a network security rule on your SAP systems, so SHIR machine can connect to them.  If your SAP system is on Azure virtual machine (VM), add the rule by setting the Source IP addresses/CIDR ranges property to your SHIR machine IP address and the Destination port ranges property to 3200,3300.  For example:

 

On SHIR machine, run the following PowerShell cmdlet to ensure that it can connect to your SAP systems: Test-NetConnection <SAP system IP address> -port 3300 

 

Prepare SAP CDC linked service

To prepare SAP CDC linked service, complete the following steps:

On ADF Studio, go to the Linked services section of Manage hub and select the New button to create a new linked service.

 

  1. Search for SAP and select SAP CDC (Preview).

 

 

  1. Set SAP CDC linked service properties, many of them are similar to SAP Table linked service properties
    1. For the Connect via integration runtime property, select your SHIR.
    2. For the Server name property, enter the mapped server name for your SAP system.
    3. For the Subscriber name property, enter a unique name to register and identify this ADF connection as a subscriber that consumes data packages produced in ODQ by your SAP system. 

 

  1. Test the connection and create your new SAP CDC linked service.

Monitor data extractions on SAP systems

To monitor data extractions on SAP systems, complete the following steps:

 

  1. Using SAP Logon Tool on your SAP source system, run ODQMON transaction code.

 

 

 

  1. Enter the value for Subcriber name property of your SAP CDC linked service in the Subscriber input field and select All in the Request Selection dropdown menu to show all data extractions using that linked service.

 

  1. You can now see all registered subscriber processes in ODQ representing data extractions from ADF copy activities that use your SAP CDC linked service.  On each ODQ subscription, you can drill down to see individual full/delta extractions.  On each extraction, you can drill down to see individual data packages that were consumed.
  2. When ADF copy activities that extract SAP data are no longer needed, their ODQ subscriptions should be deleted, so SAP systems can stop tracking their subscription states and remove the unconsumed data packages from ODQ.  To do so, select the unneeded ODQ subscriptions and delete them.

 

Conclusion:

The SAP CDC connector in Data Factory reads delta changes from the SAP ODP framework. The deltas are recorded in ODQ tables.

Reference:

Overview and architecture of the SAP CDC capabilities - Azure Data Factory | Microsoft Learn
